title: Cuisse de canard confit aux lentilles
subtitle: Crisped confit duck leg on Puy lentils with lardons and mustard
excerpt: A confit duck leg, crisped under a hot grill until the skin shatters, served on a bed of Puy lentils cooked with lardons, shallots, and a spoonful of mustard. The lentils are firm and earthy; the duck is rich. This is one of the most satisfying combinations in French cooking.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), public domain. Confit de canard method and preservation in duck fat. Henri-Paul Pellaprat, L'Art culinaire moderne (1935), public domain via archive.org. Lentilles du Puy with lardons and mustard vinaigrette as a garnish for preserved duck.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The lentils can be cooked up to 2 days ahead and refrigerated. Reheat gently with a splash of stock. Crisp the duck just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A French preservation and cooking method where meat (typically duck or goose) is salted, then cooked and stored submerged in its own rendered fat at low temperature. The fat seals out air and preserves the meat for weeks. Ready-made confit duck legs are sold in tins and vacuum packs.

content.text: duck leg is duck that has been salted, cooked gently in its own fat, and stored in that fat until needed. Ready-made confit duck legs come in tins and vacuum packs; this recipe starts from that point. Your job is to crisp the skin and build the lentil base around it.

Puy lentils are the right lentil here. They hold their shape after 25 minutes of cooking and have a slight peppery quality that works well with duck fat. Cooked with lardons and shallots, then finished with a spoonful of mustard, they are a complete dish in themselves.

The grill (or a hot dry pan) is what finishes the duck. Get it very hot. The skin crisps in 4 to 6 minutes and makes a sound like paper crackling when it is ready. Don't cover the pan or the steam will soften what you just crisped.

items.prepNote: ready-made confit duck legs, from tin or vacuum pack

content.text: A medium saucepan for the lentils and a frying pan or grill tray for the duck. The duck can be finished under the grill or in a very hot dry pan.

Warm the {{duck-fat}} of duck fat in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{lardons}} of lardons and cook for 4 minutes until beginning to colour. Add the {{shallot}} and cook for 3 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{puy-lentils}} of Puy lentils, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 22 to 25 minutes until the lentils are tender but still holding their shape. Most of the stock should be absorbed. Discard the thyme and bay. Stir in the {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard and the {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar. Season and keep warm.

Scrape any excess fat from the surface of the {{duck-legs}} confit duck legs. Heat a dry frying pan over high heat until very hot, or heat the grill to its highest setting. Place the duck legs skin-side down in the pan (or skin-side up under the grill). Cook for 5 to 6 minutes without moving until the skin is deep golden and crackling. There is no need to turn the legs; the underside is already cooked.
Spoon the lentils into wide shallow bowls. Place a crisped duck leg on top, skin-side up. Scatter over the {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley and serve at once.

items.symptom: Duck skin is soft, not crisp
items.cause: Pan or grill not hot enough, or duck skin was wet before cooking
items.fix: Pat the skin dry before cooking; make sure the pan is properly preheated. A cold pan steams the skin rather than crisping it

items.cause: Overcooked; Puy lentils cook relatively quickly
items.fix: Check at 20 minutes; they should have a little bite. If already soft, nothing can be done, but serve them anyway as they will still taste good

items.cause: Stock absorbed too quickly; lentils cooked too fast
items.fix: Add a splash more stock or water and stir. The finished lentils should be moist, not soupy, with just a little sauce remaining

items.cause: Not enough acidity; lentils need the vinegar to lift them
items.fix: Add a few more drops of red wine vinegar and another small spoon of mustard; taste as you go

content.text: Confit de canard is the great preservation dish of Gascony. Duck legs are salted and slow-cooked in their own fat; the fat seals out air and keeps the meat for weeks. Puy lentils, grown in the volcanic Auvergne, became the classic pairing in Paris bistros in the 20th century. The two regional ingredients met on the menu and proved a natural match.
